prefigures the time when he will wipe the feet of his own disciples.<|10.44|> <|12.04|> Abundance, extravagance, lavish service and devotion.<|18.76|> <|19.82|> These are all sign points of the story and of John.<|24.32|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And while this text feels like a bit much, a little bit over the top in the austerity<|6.74|> <|6.74|> of Lent, it also causes party, I hope not, but it<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But it starts with the wearing of a cross of ashes on our foreheads at the start of Lent,<|9.78|> <|11.04|> and a chorus of the A word at the end, as we don our spring clothes and go back to wine<|19.96|> <|19.96|> or chocolate or caffeine or salty language or whatever you have given up for the past<|26.02|> give as we have received, abundantly.<|9.20|> <|10.64|> And may we truly mean what we say at the dismissal of every service during the season of Lent.<|17.54|> <|19.00|> Remember the poor.<|21.22|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So falsely, Mary Magdalene has been passed down to us through history as the repentant prostitute or sinner anointing Jesus in Luke's gospel.<|16.28|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The most consistent bit of these varying stories, however, is that across all four Gospels, this one piece remains true.<|14.34|>
<|15.84|> Jesus is only ever physically anointed by a woman.<|22.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|2.00|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So why else do we think that Mary isn't just a clumsy spiller of perfume?<|8.00|>
<|9.78|> Abundance is a hallmark of John's gospel.<|14.04|>
<|15.60|> Jesus started his ministry in John in the wedding at Cana,<|20.48|>
<|20.48|> where he turned jars and jars and jars of water into the finest wine.<|27.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In John 10.10, Jesus tells the disciples in his good shepherd story that he has come, that they may have life and have it abundantly.<|12.98|>
<|14.24|> Nothing in John is stingy.<|18.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Of course Mary was intended to pour the entire pound of costly perfume made of pure nard over Jesus' feet.<|11.50|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Imagine the impact for all presence of this sweet smell of anointing and impending death with their all too recent memory of Lazarus' stench of death.<|17.80|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus' revivocation of Lazarus happened just a chapter ago.<|7.00|>
<|7.00|> And this passage points to that past.<|11.00|>
<|11.00|> It highlights their present and it prepares them for the future.<|17.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One commentator states that Lazarus' presence at that table confirms that death does not speak the final word.<|11.62|>
<|12.20|> As Jesus forges the connection between his own anointing and his own burial.<|19.54|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And Mary's wiping of Jesus' feet prefigures the time when he will wipe the feet of his own disciples.<|10.44|>
<|12.04|> Abundance, extravagance, lavish service and devotion.<|18.76|>
<|19.82|> These are all sign points of the story and of John.<|24.32|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And while this text feels like a bit much, a little bit over the top in the austerity<|6.74|>
<|6.74|> of Lent, it also causes us to pause and think about how uncomfortable we can make others<|17.18|>
<|17.18|> when we overtly express our faith.<|20.14|>
<|21.16|> It may not be the wiping of feet with our hair at a dinner party, I hope not, but it<|28.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But it starts with the wearing of a cross of ashes on our foreheads at the start of Lent,<|9.78|>
<|11.04|> and a chorus of the A word at the end, as we don our spring clothes and go back to wine<|19.96|>
<|19.96|> or chocolate or caffeine or salty language or whatever you have given up for the past<|26.02|>
